meta.name: THE FROZEN GALLERY
meta.tagline: Time stopped here. The dead are preserved perfectly.
meta.mechanicDescription: Chill stacks reduce stamina regen. Freeze enemies to skip their turn. Ice enemies immune.

lore: Something exhaled once, deep beneath the named places, and everything stopped. The flames are still burning inside the walls. The dead are still wearing their expressions. Whatever it was that exhaled has not inhaled yet.

opening: The cold arrives before the light does, settling into your lungs with the patience of something that has been waiting in this passage for a long time.
Your breath clouds and does not disperse. The air is too still to carry it anywhere. You watch it hang and thin and disappear, and the silence holds.
Nothing smells here. The cold has killed it — whatever rot, whatever mineral, whatever living thing would announce itself in another zone. Here there is only absence.
Your footsteps ring out and stop. The stone floor is covered in frost so fine it looks like breath has been laid across the surface. You leave prints behind you. You are the only moving thing.
The Gallery is lit from inside the walls. Torches sealed in ice decades ago still burn — amber, unflickering, casting light that reaches for you and fails to arrive warm.
Cold presses against your face like a hand. Not pushing — just present. The kind of cold that doesn't ask you to move faster.
Silence this complete has texture. You hear the blood moving in your own ears. You hear your heartbeat. You hear the small sounds of your own presence, which are the only sounds there are.
The frost on the walls is not a coating. It is a record — layer upon layer of cold seasons, each pressing deeper. You can see the years in cross-section where the ice has cracked.
Metal would stick to your bare fingers here. You know this without testing it. The air carries that warning in its weight, in the way it presses back when you breathe against it.
At the threshold, the cold is a wall you have to step through. On the other side: silence deeper than anything above, and the particular stillness of a place that has not been disturbed in years.
The light through the ice walls is amber and complete, the way candlelight is — but no candle moves. Nothing here moves. The Gallery keeps everything exactly as it was.
The temperature change is the first warning. Not a draft — there are no drafts here — but a settling, as if the air itself has grown heavier with cold.
middle: A figure stands at the far wall with its back to you, one arm raised as if pointing at something in the corner. It has been pointing for longer than you have been alive. Its coat is still good.
The writing on the wall is mid-sentence. The stylus lies on the floor beneath it, dropped rather than set down. Whatever the scholar was writing, the Gallery answered before the sentence ended.
Two figures face each other across a low surface, mouths open, hands extended — mid-argument, mid-word, stopped in the instant between accusation and response. Their expressions are still legible.
A child's boot near the far passage. Then the child — frozen against the wall, hands covering its face, as if the act of not seeing might have helped. The hands are still perfect.
The torches in the sealed walls cast no shadow you can stand in. Every angle is lit. You can see every face. None of them look at you. You check twice.
A figure here was in motion when it stopped. Weight forward, arms behind, every line of the body saying forward. It went nowhere. The Gallery is patient about that.
Frost has built up on the shoulders of the figure in the alcove — years of accretion, a geological record of how long it has been waiting there. The layers are very thick.

The Gallery holds a room of pilgrims preserved mid-prayer, hands raised, heads bowed, lips parted around syllables that will never finish. The prayer has been waiting for an answer for longer than the language it was spoken in has existed.

An open book on a reading stand, pages turned to a chapter on the zone's early history. The reader is still here — seated, one finger on the page. They were pleased by something they found. It shows on their face.

The ice in the far wall has preserved something in perfect clarity: a face pressed against the inside of it, eyes open, looking out. Looking at this room. Looking at whatever comes through here. Looking at you.
Handwriting on ice — not scratched but pressed, as if someone wrote on the surface while it was still soft and the cold preserved the impression. The letters are careful. Someone was not hurrying.
A pile of equipment near the center — pack, tools, weapon — and no one attached to them. Whoever dropped these ran. The Gallery caught them somewhere else.
closing: The cold does not threaten. It waits. It has always been better at waiting than you.
Whatever happened here happened all at once. The room holds the evidence of a single instant. The next instant has been delayed for a very long time.
The Gallery keeps its dead in better condition than the living manage. That fact settles over you like another degree of cold.
You are the warmest thing in this room by a significant margin. The Gallery has noticed.
Behind you, the passage shows your footprints in the frost. Nothing else disturbs the surface. But the cold does not need to disturb surfaces to move.
There is nowhere to hurry to here. The Gallery has all the time in the world. So do the things it keeps.
The silence is not empty. It is full of something you haven't located yet. You move toward the next passage. The silence follows at the same distance.
You leave the room and the figures remain in their positions, as they have always remained. They do not watch you go. They do not need to.
Every surface here carries the impression of something that was here before the freeze. The Gallery forgets nothing. It has not forgotten you arrived.
Warmth is not available in the Gallery. You are managing a supply of it that is being spent, incrementally, with every minute you stay.
The things preserved here died without warning. They had no time to brace. They had no time to know. The Gallery teaches this lesson to everyone who enters. Eventually.
The cold that stopped this place was absolute. It did not negotiate. It did not warn. The Gallery stands as proof of what happens when it decides.

opening: The temperature in this room drops the moment you enter it. A degree, then two, then something that makes your fingers ache through the wrappings.
The room holds dozens of preserved figures and you know, without being able to say how, that the count is wrong. One of them is not frozen.
Your breath fogs and vanishes. Then you notice: the frost on the floor nearest the far wall is uneven. Something has stood there recently. Something that displaced the ice as it moved.
The stillness here is different from the stillness in other rooms. Not the stillness of preservation. The stillness of something that has stopped moving because you arrived.
Ice cracks — a single sound, directional, and then nothing. The sound came from inside the room, not the walls. Not the ceiling. From the floor, where something was lying.
The Gallery holds its cold in layers, each room a slightly different register. This room is colder than the ones before it. Not from depth. From presence.
The preserved figures along the far wall are facing a different direction than they were when you passed the window into this room. You are certain of it. Forty seconds ago, they faced away.
Something in this room still has warmth in it. Not much. Just enough to disturb the air, to create the faint differential that the cold exploits. You feel it before you see it.
The frost on the ceiling falls in a fine powder — not from settling, but from displacement. Something moved in here not long ago. The frost is still drifting.

The sound of ice under compression fills the room — that deep, structural complaint of weight shifting, of something massive finding new equilibrium. Then the sound stops. Then the first figure moves.
You entered rooms like this before and found them empty. This room is not empty. The difference is in how the silence sits — not the silence of absence but the silence of attention.
The amber torchlight throws shadows that should be stationary. One of them is not. One of them moved a fraction of a second after the thing that cast it.
middle: The Preserved reaches toward you. Not threatening — reaching. The way something reaches toward the only warm thing in a very cold room. Its coat is still good. Someone's name is stitched inside the collar.
The Ice Wraith has no face. Just the shape of wanting, taking cold form, pressing through the space between you. Where it passes, the air temperature drops and does not recover. It is circling.
The Frost Sentinel has been standing at this threshold since before the Gallery froze. The ice on its shoulders is layered like stone strata. It moves now, for the first time in decades, toward you.

Two of The Preserved. Three. They were pilgrims once — you can see what they were carrying when they stopped. The ice released just enough grip that they can move again, slowly, toward the warmth. Toward you.

The Wraith passes through the wall and the temperature in the room falls five degrees in the time it takes you to turn. It is not attacking. It is feeding. The distinction matters less than it should.

The Shattered moves wrong — not like a body, but like debris. Four pieces coordinating, then five, then more as the fight continues. The floor is getting crowded with parts of something that used to be whole.

The Frost Sentinel's visor reflects your face. Your reflection turns its head before you do. The ice on its armor is thick enough to deflect most things. There is no most here. There is only what you have.

Ice Wraiths don't announce themselves. They arrive as a temperature differential, a sudden drain, a CHILL settling into joints you didn't notice were warm until they stopped being warm. There are two of them. At least.

The Preserved advanced slowly enough that you almost didn't register the motion. Now it is close. It carries the cold of decades in its joints, in its hands, in the expression it wore the moment the Gallery took it.
The Frost Sentinel was dormant. You came close enough. Now it moves — grinding, slow, each step a geological event. The ice on its joints doesn't slow it. It just makes it louder.
The Shattered was frozen once and cracked. Each piece moves independently, separately hungry. Attacking the largest piece creates two smaller ones. The room is filling up with parts.

The Preserved here came in groups — pilgrims, scholars, the merely lost. They thaw toward you together. None of them look afraid. They look like people who have been very cold for a very long time and have just now registered your warmth.
closing: There is no way around. Only through. The cold is watching to see how you manage it.
It has not hurried. It will not hurry. The Gallery does not need to.
The temperature has been dropping since you entered this room. You need to finish this before you lose more than you can recover.
Whatever it was before the Gallery took it, it is cold now, and cold tends toward warmth.
The Sentinel was placed here. Something decided this threshold required guarding. That something has not changed its mind.
The wraiths have circled long enough. They have found the gap in your warmth. They are moving toward it.
The preserved have no urgency. They have been patient for longer than you can measure. They will be patient for as long as this takes. You cannot afford to match their patience.
The cold does not take sides. But you are the only warm thing in this room, and warmth is what everything here is reaching for.
Ice creaks overhead. The Gallery registers the combat the way a building registers an earthquake — structural acknowledgment, nothing more. It has held through worse. You are smaller than worse.
Still. Watching. Cold.

framing: The cold preserved them perfectly. You cannot look at this body and see decay. You can only see a person who has stopped, mid-everything, in perfect condition.
The Gallery keeps its dead better than any burial. The body here is more intact than any corpse you have found before — more intact, you realize, than you have any right to be uncomfortable with.
Frost covers them, but not obscuring. The ice is a medium, not a veil. The expression, the set of the hands, the direction of the gaze — all of it preserved. All of it available to you.
Nothing has decomposed here. The cold forbids it. What lies before you is not a reminder of a person — it is the person, stopped, in the middle of being alive.
They look like they could wake. The color in their face is not the color of death but the color of cold sleep. You know they will not wake. You will keep checking anyway.
The Gallery took them gently, as far as you can tell. The expression is not of pain. The cold came fast enough that the only thing preserved is the moment before they knew.

Decades of freezing have not changed them. The clothes are the same. The face is the same. The boots are still good. Someone, somewhere far above, is going to miss these boots. They do not know they should have already started.

Preserved so well that you notice which pocket has a hole in it. Which button is loose. Which boot is tied tighter than the other. You know them better from five seconds of observation than from any length of memory.

The cold did not take them slowly. This one was caught mid-motion — the body still angled with forward momentum, frozen in the geometry of a stride that never finished. The Gallery caught them in transit.

Ice has formed around them over the years, incorporating them slowly into the Gallery's structure. They are part of the wall now — one arm still extended, fingers distinct, reaching into the room as if asking for something that has not arrived.
discoveryBeats: A shape in the ice near the east wall. Not a natural formation. Too regular. Upright. The amber torchlight shows something inside it, and your eyes adjust, and it is a face.
The boot, first — one boot in the passage, sitting upright as if placed there deliberately. Then the ankle above it. Then the rest, folded against the wall.

The fog of your breath drifts and shows you something you missed on first scan: a hand extended from an alcove at shoulder height. The fingers are still separate. They are not reaching toward you. They are pointing.
The silence changes quality before you find the body — becomes heavier, more considered. As if the room has been holding something and is finally releasing it to your attention.
The frost on the floor is disturbed in a pattern that stops abruptly — one set of footprints walking in, stopping, and then no indication of what happened next except the shape against the far wall.

Something at the periphery of the torchlight. The amber catches it differently — not the stone color of the walls, but the softer look of preserved fabric, of something that was once worn by someone who chose it.
The writing on the wall near the floor catches you first. Then you see what is sitting beneath the writing, and you understand who put it there.

They are upright. Seated. Back straight. Hands in lap. You almost do not recognize it as a body, because the posture is so deliberately composed. Someone arranged themselves before the cold completed its work.

A name, scratched into the ice at eye level. Then below it: a date. Then below the date: the beginning of a sentence. Then below the sentence: the rest of the story, and the body that needed to tell it.
Your light catches something small on the floor: a ring, removed and set aside. Personal. Deliberate. A choice made at the end. Then you find the person who made it.

locationLines: A sealed side chamber, its door cracked open by decades of glacial movement, the interior dry and cold and exactly as whoever left it arranged it.
Above the frost line — a shallow alcove carved into the stone, out of reach of the Gallery's lowest temperatures. Someone found this spot and understood its value.
A room that was sealed when the Gallery froze, still sealed until now, opened by the weight of time pressing stone against ice and finding a way through. The provisions inside are as they were.
Ice-preserved containers stacked against the far wall — labeled in a hand that was careful and unhurried. The contents are intact. The same cold that destroyed everything else protected these.
A narrow passage above the main corridor, accessible from a shelf of subsided stone. The Gallery's geometry created it. Someone found it. Someone left things here.
The Archivist's station: a work surface of compressed ice, covered in frost-cured paper, supplies arranged with the care of someone who has been maintaining this route for a very long time.

A thermal pocket where geothermal warmth seeps through a crack in the floor — the ice here has receded, creating a small space several degrees above the Gallery's constant. Supplies have been left in the relative warmth.

Behind a figure that has been standing in this alcove so long you almost miss the cache beside it — the figure is part of the Gallery's collection now, immovable, facing away from what it spent its death protecting.
toneClosers: The cold does not leave this room. Safety, in the Gallery, is a matter of degree.
Rest here. The Gallery will be exactly as you left it when you continue. It does not change while you wait. It does not need to.
Nothing moves. Nothing watches. And yet the Gallery's attention, which is the attention of cold patient stone, does not leave you even here.
Whoever left these supplies did not leave. They are somewhere in the Gallery, preserved, perfectly intact, waiting as all things here wait.
The silence in a cache room is the same silence as everywhere else in the Gallery. The supplies are different. The silence is identical.
Rest is possible. Warmth is not. Take what helps and remember that every minute spent here is a minute the cold has to work on the warmth you came in with.
Safe enough. The Gallery has not taken anyone from a room like this — not that you know of. You know very little about what the Gallery has or hasn't taken.
The cold is patient. It is most patient when you are still.

opening: A warmth at the far edge of the passage — not temperature, exactly, but the memory of temperature. The Gallery's certainty falters here. The cold thins.

Light that is not amber. Not the trapped torchlight of the Gallery's sealed walls — different light, from above, falling through a crack in the stone ceiling and landing on the frost without warming it.

The ice in the walls here is older, the sealing complete. But ahead, where the passage bends, the quality of the cold changes — less absolute, less patient. The surface is close enough to complicate it.

A door you did not expect. Wood, impossibly warm to the touch — warped against the cold, the grain expanded, but holding. On the other side you feel it: something different from everything behind you.

The frost on the floor shows something unusual: it stops. A precise line where the Gallery's absolute preservation ends and something less certain begins. The cold still holds there. It just doesn't hold as well.

Your breath still clouds, but it moves now — the first air movement you've felt since entering. A draft from above. The surface pressing down against the Gallery's cold and finding it is not as immovable as it was.

Cracking sounds overhead — not structural, not the glacier groaning, but something more deliberate. Ice separating from stone. The pressure of outside temperature working through from above, following whatever flaw it found.
The Gallery shows you the end the way it has shown you everything: without warning, without announcement. The passage simply opens into something lighter. The cold simply becomes less. It is enough.
middle: The figures in the last room are still in their positions. You cannot see them from here, but you know they are there, still preserving their moment. The Gallery keeps everything you've seen. It will keep the memory of your passing.

The cold releases you the way a held breath releases — not warmth arriving but pressure departing. Something vast and patient registered your presence from the moment you entered. It registers your leaving now.

Behind you, the Gallery continues. The Archivist is still recording. The Preserved are still moving toward the warmth you've left behind. The oldest ice still holds what the first cold captured. You leave all of it.

The passage rises and the temperature rises with it, degree by degree, each step a negotiation between the Gallery's certainty and the surface's disorder. The Gallery does not fight this. It simply holds what it holds, which is everything below.

You pass the frost line. The air changes character — less absolute, less preserved. You can smell something now: stone, mineral, the faint suggestion of above. The Gallery's silence recedes but does not disappear.

The exit was always here. The Gallery knew about it. The Gallery knows about everything inside it. It chose to release you, or you chose well enough that the release was earned. Either way, the way opens.

What you leave behind: the figures in the outer hall. A scholar's mid-sentence. The boots of someone whose name is stitched into the collar. The expression that a scholar wore when they understood something. All of it still there. None of it coming with you.
The cold does not chase you up the passage. It does not need to. The Gallery is patient, and patient things understand the value of waiting for someone to return.
closing: The Gallery lets you go. For now.
The surface is above you and the cold is below you and you carry a piece of each, forever, from this point forward.
You will be warm again. The warmth will be strange. You have learned what cold is capable of. That knowledge stays.
The Gallery has been here longer than you have existed. It will be here after. You are a brief warmth it held for a moment and released. It will not forget you. Cold forgets nothing.
The exit closes behind you — not a sound, not a mechanism, just the Gallery's returning silence. It knows where you are. It knows you'll be back.
Whatever you're carrying out of the Gallery — loot, wounds, the faces of the preserved — the cold comes with it. Check your joints in the morning. It stays.
You survived. The Gallery is not surprised. Pilgrims sometimes survive. They come back.
The cold was here before you and will remain after. You passed through it. It passed through you. The exchange is permanent.

local.name: The Preserved
local.description: Frozen pilgrims thawed just enough to move. They reach toward warmth with the patience of things that have waited decades for it. They remember nothing. They move because cold things thaw toward heat.

local.name: Ice Wraiths
local.description: Hunger given cold shape. They pass through walls and leave temperature differentials behind. Contact applies CHILL. They are not attacking you. They are feeding.

local.name: Frost Sentinels
local.description: Ancient guardians sealed in ice-compressed armor. Dormant until approached. Immune to FREEZE. Their steps are geological events. Their single strike drops the air temperature in the room.

local.name: The Shattered
local.description: Frozen things that cracked rather than held. Each fragment moves independently. Damaging the whole creates more pieces. The room fills with debris that is still hungry.

local.name: The Glacial Sovereign
local.description: The first thing frozen. It was here before the Gallery had a name, before the first pilgrim descended. It moves with the certainty of geological time. Each strike drops the room temperature. It has been waiting for warmth since before you existed. Immune to FREEZE.

depths.name: THE OUTER GALLERY
depths.description: The entrance halls. Figures frozen in welcome.

depths.name: THE COLLECTION
depths.description: Deeper preservation. The figures here died slower.

depths.name: THE VAULT
depths.description: The oldest ice. The first things frozen.
